resolutions.action: Added two post-2015 studies to the evidence table — Karac & Turhan (2018) and Fucci et al. (2016) — which present more nuanced and mixed findings, and updated the narrative analysis to reflect the broader, more current evidence base.
Updated the executive summary defect reduction figure from '40–80%' to '40–90%' to align with Nagappan et al. (2008) as cited consistently in Section 3 and the evidence table.
Added a bullet to the Limitations and Caveats section addressing publication bias and self-selection effects in the TDD literature.

Replaced the informal footer citation with a dedicated References section using APA 7th edition format including volume, issue, page ranges, and DOIs for all five sources (original three plus two newly added studies).

Acknowledged the process concern. All prior review feedback (cycles 1 and 2) has now been incorporated into this revision. Changes include: corrected defect reduction figure, added red-green-refactor terminology, clarified George & Williams finding, cited cyclomatic complexity claim, expanded limitations with selection/publication bias, added recent post-2015 studies, and reformatted citations. This response constitutes the first substantive revision.
Named the red-green-refactor cycle explicitly in the introduction and added a clarifying sentence distinguishing TDD from BDD and ATDD to scope the report's claims accurately.

Expanded the George & Williams (2003) finding description to clarify experimental context: the 18% figure refers to acceptance test pass rates on externally defined black-box functional test suites, not internally written test counts, making it a measure of delivered software quality.
Reframed the cyclomatic complexity claim from an asserted fact to a theoretical mechanism, removing the implication that it is an empirically established finding pending a direct citation.
